title: The Macro Minute: Will the BOJ blow up the 42 Macro Paradigm C bull market?

Happy Friday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dell to
present our macro minute for Friday,
January 23rd, 2026. As always, we'll
start with the executive summary from
today's leadoff morning note. So, let's
dive right in. Today's key macro
question is, will the BOJ blow up the 42
macro paradigm C bull market? The short
answer is not likely, but Japan's
Ministry of Finance may do so if the
Takahayichi government fails to heed the
warning in the currency market. Terms of
analytical nuance, the Bank of Japan
left its benchmark policy rate unchanged
at 0.75% that open up the door to
another hike in April, likely to stem
the tide of selling in the JGB market,
where yields exploded to 30-year highs
this week. In leaving the door open for
April, the BOJ cited the need to
evaluate the effects of the prior
month's increase and to await the
outcome of a snap election that could
reshape fiscal spending. The rate
decision was unanim unanimously
anticipated by economists and keeps
overnight borrowing costs at their
highest level in roughly 30 years as
well. The decision carried hawkish
undertones via higher inflation forecast
and a dissenting vote from BOJ board
member Hajime Takata favoring an
immediate hike. Earlier this month, the
Japanese yen touched a fresh 18-month
trough at 159 per US dollar following
reports that Prime Minister Sai
Takayichi would call an election. A
victory would entrench her parliamentary
coalition and enable more aggressive
fiscal expansion, which would add
downward pressure on the Japanese yen
and upward pressure on JGB yields.
Takaichi's proposed tax changes followed
the premier's passage last month of an
18 trillion yen, $122 billion economic
stimulus packaged aimed at reducing
utility and gasoline costs. Despite
this, the BOJ raised its inflation
forecast on expectations that these
measures will lift underlying price
pressures over the long term. This may
be because Joe Biden's historically
failed presidency recently confirmed
that fiscal authorities cannot spend
their way out of an inflation problem.
Duh. Money markets are currently pricing
the BJ to be far and away the world's
most hawkish central bank over uh over
the next year. Sorry, the ma most
hawkish major central bank over the next
year. Uh the BOJ's relatively hawkish
posture has done little to support the
Japanese yen. The BOJ is also tightening
with this balance sheet, which has
contracted 11% since peaking in mid
2024. The contraction in the BOJ's
balance sheet is weighing heavily on
Japanese liquidity, which is contracting
22% on a 3-month annualized basis. The
Japanese yen is much weaker than the
BJ's balance sheet policy, and real
interest rate differentials would imply,
which signals the existence of a fiscal
policy discount. If market participants
are currently assigning a fiscal policy
discount to Japan, one of the world's
largest net international investment
surplus economies, then they will likely
eventually apply a fiscal policy
discount to the US and the US dollar
because the US is far and away the
world's largest net international
investment deficit economy, particularly
if we're right on structural reforms at
the Fed, which would ultimately allow
the US to have a a durably negative
floor a real floor Fed funds rate. Uh
and so if we had a durably negative
short policy rate uh in the context of a
fiscal policy discount, you know, you
can really open up some downside in the
dollar. And so finally, a durable
erosion of Fed independence represents a
durable headwind for the US dollar given
the US's poor fiscal outlook. Uh which
we obviously have hit on for for quite a
while now. uh you know I think we're you
know we we provided some of the world's
best thought leadership of the past half
decade uh when we authored our view that
there's a growing geopolitically driven
supply demand imbalance in the US
Treasury bond market and that gold is
the only is the best diversifier away
from that supply demand imbalance
because the Fed is the only institution
in the world with a balance sheet large
and flexible enough to fill that void
and so that void is going to get bigger
and bigger and bigger over time and
you're going to need more and more and
more uh structural reforms at the Fed
that allow it to essentially uh support
the Treasury bond market uh which would
ultimately you know essentially equate
to an erosion of of of Fed independence.
So that continues to be a very high
probability outcome from the perspective
of our datadriven uh research
particularly our investing during a for
regime framework uh which we authored
back in the summer of 2023. So as always
members of our global investor community
can trust that our institutional grade
riskmanagement overlays Kiss and Dr. Mo
will help our portfolios navigate these
emergent risks better than investors
attempting to manage risk without
systematic rules or relying on
fundamental research alone.
Transitioning to our 42 micro dashboard
as always wrap up with a question from
our community. This one's titled more AI
skepticism says Ken Griffin who I
respect a lot as a public commentator.
You should the man's built one of the
best companies in history certainly one
of the best uh financial companies in
history on the asset management side. uh
Citadel uh came out hard against the AI
productivity boom basically call it all
hype. However, which is I find uh that's
a lie. I can I know with great great
confidence that Citadel is experiencing
a productivity boom. I won't tell you
why or how I know that, but trust me, I
know. Uh however, he makes a great
observation that I haven't seen anyone
else point out, which is that the AI
hype is allowing company managements to
uh uh company management cover to make
real productivity gains by investing
aggressively in regular non-AI existing
proven technologies. Uh so uh let me
take the step. So one I I I don't think
Ken Griffin is is believes uh that we're
an AI productivity. The AI productivity
boom is all hype. Um you can just look
at their returns. obviously had to have
been long a lot of these companies uh in
their in their funds. Two, uh we're
already in a productivity boom. By the
way, productivity growth in the third
quarter of of last year, which is the
last quarter we have the data for. It
expanded at a 4.8% annualized rate uh uh
in Q3 and a 4.5% annualized rate on a
two quarter on a six-month annualized
basis. Again, productivity growth is
somewhere close to four and a half to 5%
whether you look at it on a three-month
or six-month annualized basis. And that
compares to a compares to a a long a
trend level of about 2% just slightly
below 2%. So obviously we're not going
to stay at four to 5% in productivity
growth terms but we're on record uh
calling for a structural uptrend in
productivity that leads the trend
productivity rate over the next 5 to 10
years somewhere around 3%. We've already
seen this happen before uh in the uh uh
internet uh um in terms of the internet
capex build out and the and resulting
productivity we had from the diffusion
of internet technology throughout the
economy. This technology is way more
powerful than the internet. It can do
your job for you. You have to use the
internet to do your job. AI when trained
properly can literally do your job for
you. And so productivity is output per
hour worked. If hours worked goes down
because we have less people working and
output stays the same andor goes up
because machines are generally better
than us at doing stuff, then we will
have a productivity boom. that is that
that that if you're an investor, you
must understand that that is one of the
highest probability outcomes. Now, we
may have some snafoos getting there.
Energy could be an issue. Social or
social unrest could be an issue in terms
of pushing back on that. But in in my
opinion, we think uh you're probably
going to see a continuation of the a
structural uptrend in productivity
growth until we get to a point where we
have bottlenecks from an energy
perspective and until we get to the
point where we have uh social unrest as
a function of job displacement. uh but
those in my opinion are secondary. Those
are second order impacts. The first
order impact is the diffusion of the
technology throughout the economy
creates higher productivity growth. And
by the way, we're already seeing it in
the data. So um getting back into the
second part of the question, u the
product the the the uh the AI hype is
allowing company managements to make
real productivity gains by aggressing
investing aggressively in regular non AI
existing proven technologies.
I I don't know if that's right or wrong.
I that's not something I've looked at uh
had a time to look at. But the reality
is what difference does it make? If
you're investing in AI, investing in
nonAI technologies that both wind up in
the same place with more productivity
growth on a trend basis, that's
incredibly bullish. It's so bullish for
corporate profits. It's so uh the
disinflationary uh you're talking and
then disinflation equals higher
multiples like this is it people if
you're in a productivity renaissance,
you have to be bullish until further
